full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Apostle Paul is not a storyteller.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> I've been doing a Bible study on 1 Corinthians this whole year,<|9.00|> <|9.00|> and really the most interesting part of the Bible study<|12.00|> <|12.00|> and what we've found just so interesting<|15.00|> <|15.00|> and that we've debated and argued about why this is the case,<|19.00|> <|19.00|> but is that Paul doesn't tell any stories about the life of Jesus.<|26.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.84|> And think about this just for a moment.<|1.84|> <|2.04|> The Gospels, Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John, are all about stories, right?<|6.56|> <|6.96|> The stories of the healing of the woman's daughter who was sick.<|13.34|> <|13.34|> The story of the feeding of the 5,000.<|17.20|> <|17.98|> All these stories, the stories of resurrection.<|21.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.88|> The stories of Jesus eating with people and so forth.<|4.52|> <|4.64|> Paul doesn't tell a single one of those stories.<|6.86|> <|7.32|> Paul talks a lot about the cross, even more, thankfully, about the resurrection and what it all means.<|15.08|> <|15.18|> But no stories.<|16.30|> <|16.60|> And we don't know why that is.<|17.96|> <|18.02|> Did he know the stories and not tell them?<|19.82|> <|19.88|> That's perplexing.<|20.86|> <|20.86|> Did he not know them?<|22.10|> <|22.18|> That actually is a possibility.<|23.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> fascinating. segments.text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The Apostle Paul is not a storyteller.<|5.00|>
<|5.00|> I've been doing a Bible study on 1 Corinthians this whole year,<|9.00|>
<|9.00|> and really the most interesting part of the Bible study<|12.00|>
<|12.00|> and what we've found just so interesting<|15.00|>
<|15.00|> and that we've debated and argued about why this is the case,<|19.00|>
<|19.00|> but is that Paul doesn't tell any stories about the life of Jesus.<|26.40|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.84|> And think about this just for a moment.<|1.84|>
<|2.04|> The Gospels, Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John, are all about stories, right?<|6.56|>
<|6.96|> The stories of the healing of the woman's daughter who was sick.<|13.34|>
<|13.34|> The story of the feeding of the 5,000.<|17.20|>
<|17.98|> All these stories, the stories of resurrection.<|21.10|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.88|> The stories of Jesus eating with people and so forth.<|4.52|>
<|4.64|> Paul doesn't tell a single one of those stories.<|6.86|>
<|7.32|> Paul talks a lot about the cross, even more, thankfully, about the resurrection and what it all means.<|15.08|>
<|16.60|> And we don't know why that is.<|17.96|>
<|18.02|> Did he know the stories and not tell them?<|19.82|>
<|19.88|> That's perplexing.<|20.86|>
<|20.86|> Did he not know them?<|22.10|>
<|22.18|> That actually is a possibility.<|23.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> fascinating. What Paul was trying to do was in places like Corinth, which is a Greek city,<|7.56|>
<|8.34|> Paul is this apostle, this missionary mind, and he's trying to translate<|13.22|>
<|13.22|> the stories of Christ, a lot of the Jewish stories that are in the background of Christ.<|19.82|>
<|19.82|> He's trying to translate all of that, what he knows of the teachings of Jesus and<|24.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> into some kind of meaning for people who don't have presuppositions about the God of Abraham and Sarah and so forth.<|9.60|>
<|9.66|> He's trying to translate it and get it in a nutshell for people who don't have that as a starting point.<|14.64|>
<|15.32|> So he doesn't make recourse to stories, which is probably why most of us, certainly me, aren't quite so drawn to Paul.<|24.50|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> You know, I tend to preach on the four gospels, not so much on Paul.<|4.12|>
<|4.24|> But this Sunday, when Paul finally makes it to 1 Corinthians 13,<|8.72|>
<|9.00|> when he finally makes it to what he believes it all means,<|12.40|>
<|13.50|> he does something poetic and so powerful and succinct.<|17.74|>
<|18.40|> He, in a nutshell, tells us what it all means and even what it all feels like,<|23.52|>
<|23.60|> this passage on love.<|25.88|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Love is patient.<|3.00|>
<|6.18|> Love is not envious or boastful or arrogant or rude.<|12.72|>
<|14.86|> And then love never ends.<|18.24|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> it's what it's all about<|3.46|>
<|3.46|> not just this moment<|4.60|>
<|4.60|> but all what's on the other side<|7.00|>
<|7.00|> it's what it's all about<|8.22|>
<|8.22|> for Paul here<|9.40|>
<|9.40|> love is not just sort of one option<|11.96|>
<|11.96|> among many other options<|13.48|>
<|13.48|> it's not one tool in our toolbox<|15.48|>
<|15.48|> it's not an episode<|18.02|>
<|18.02|> in which we kind of try to love<|20.18|>
<|20.18|> it's the whole thing<|21.86|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.02|> It's for each breath.<|2.80|>
<|3.66|> It's for each step of this journey.<|5.44|>
<|6.52|> I've done so many weddings for 20 years as a priest<|9.40|>
<|9.40|> that have picked this as the reading<|12.66|>
<|12.66|> that I have to remind myself<|14.62|>
<|14.62|> that Paul didn't write this for couples.<|16.48|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Paul is writing this passage about what it means to be the body of Christ,<|6.62|>
<|6.74|> what it means to be the church, what it means to be the Christian community.<|10.34|>
<|11.34|> This is our vision statement.<|13.12|>
<|14.18|> This is our mission.<|15.50|>
<|16.24|> This is our culture.<|17.50|>
<|18.08|> This is our goal.<|19.34|>
<|19.82|> When we get it right, it's because we love.<|22.08|>
<|22.30|> This is the whole thing.<|23.88|>
<|23.88|> This is our mission.<|24.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> love was his meaning, as Julian of Norwich put it.<|4.48|>

<|10.64|> to do my report is a part of the sermon at all of the services.<|16.84|>
<|17.54|> And because this is our passage, it's really a report on love.<|20.80|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We have loved a lot in the past year, and we've learned a lot.<|5.60|>

<|6.14|> I knew our doctors and nurses and social workers and our administrators,<|11.34|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> There's a, I've been told that an Archbishop of Canterbury once said, but I've never been able to find it, but it doesn't matter, said, if you don't have a sense of humor, you don't have a sense of proportion and you should never be put in charge of anything.<|15.90|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And I thank Lee, Michael, Shirley, and Tom for their sense of proportion.<|7.74|>
<|8.70|> It's remarkable and means a great deal to me.<|11.16|>
<|13.66|> God's will for us is that we love one another and that we love our neighbors as ourselves.<|21.92|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But do you know what's even more amazing and mysterious?<|5.68|>
<|8.98|> It's not just that God's will is for us to love, but that God is love.<|16.74|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In the words of 1 John, that God is love.<|6.76|>
